Alright, you're back to your next lesson so let's rock on. This is an absolutely crucial one, how to get past the gatekeeper. Here's the deal. Whether you're cold calling on the phone or out there banging on doors in the field, at the end of the day what you're going to find is that in the real world of sales, your ability to get past the gatekeeper and speak to the decision maker is going to be instrumental to your success. In fact, let's go back to the graphic of the sales funnel I created in the previous lesson. We'll take one from Strictly Phone-Based Illustrator Point. Now notice here that when we actually make contact there's only 50 human beings we spoke to. Start with 100 but only 50 people actually picked up the phone, right? But of those 50 people, only 25 of them were actual decision makers. You lost half right in that one step. Now while there are different reasons for that, the most common one by far, especially when you're calling on businesses, has to do with the day-to-day challenge you'll face of getting past gatekeepers to speak to the decision makers so you can then use the straight line system and close the deal. Now that being said, because the strategies of getting past gatekeepers are very different for over the phone versus in person, I'm going to give you three different options here. Hell, before you make your choice, I want you to consider two very important things. First, if you choose both, you're going to have about an 18-minute chunk of content to go through which is a bit on the long side in terms of best adult learning practice. However, if you have the time, it is definitely worth it. So choose that one. Number two, if you're trying to get through a high-value target, meaning that when you close one, there'll be a huge commission. Well even if you're doing that strictly by phone, I cover many of those strategies in the in-person section, so please choose both for that as well. So with that in mind, do you want to get past gatekeepers on the phone? Do you want to get past gatekeepers out in the field in person? Would you like to learn both right now? Choose whichever one makes sense for you. Do it right now. Okay, great. It shows the telephone. Remember, please go through the other modality as well, meaning in-person methodologies for getting past gatekeepers, just in case you find yourself in that situation in the future. So I place that portion of this training in with your exercises. So go through that as well when you have time. Make sense? Okay, great. So here's the deal. After all my years in selling, after spending 30 plus years teaching countless companies and countless salespeople how to get past gatekeepers when they're calling on the phone, the strategy that works better than anything else out there by a country mile is to use what I call ethical subterfuge to persuade the gatekeeper into thinking that you're either a personal friend of the decision maker or a current business associate. That's it. You get it? That's it. That's the only way to get past ethical subterfuge. Well, basically it would be very unethical to say to the gatekeeper, hi, I'm a personal friend of John Smith, I'm his brother, I'm his uncle. I don't ever want you to do anything even remotely like that. However, there's a certain way of sounding, a certain way of carrying yourself over the phone that will allow you to be perceived exactly that way. Because I never ever want you to lie. For example, let's say you try to get through to Mr. John Smith from XYZ Corporation and you call up and say, hi, my name is Jordan Delfort. I work for ABC Corporation. Is Mr. Smith in? The chance of getting past that gatekeeper are zero. They know you're a salesperson from the moment you open up your mouth that formal tonality used coupled with those equally formal words is just an overwatching. You're done. Don't be prized, so to speak, and you never get past the gatekeeper. Now, while that formal greeting of, hi, I'm Jordan Delfort, it is Mr. Bob Smith there. While that's the most toxic greeting you could possibly use to try to get through to a gatekeeper, let me give you the other end of the spectrum in terms of the most effective one. Hey, is Bob around? That's it. Hey, is Bob there? Bob, please. You wanna ask for the boss, for the decision maker in the same way that you would ask him if he was your best friend and use that formal tone, hey, is Bob around? Bob, please. And then you shut up and don't say a word. This is called creating poisonous silence. You see, while gatekeepers have been trained very well in stopping you from getting past and using words, what they have not been trained at is how to handle no words at all. So when you actually shut up and there's silence, they don't know what to do. Now remember from tonality, that spinning wheel of death you create with the right tonality, right? So when you essentially paralyze there in a model law, what is that gatekeeper thinking right now? They're like, oh boy, I don't wanna get in trouble because this might be someone that's important to him, or maybe someone I should know because he sounded so familiar, and they'll just say, hold on a second, that's what you'll get, and they'll go back to the boss. Now if the boss is in a talkative mood, you caught the guy basically in the right frame of mind, he'll just come right on the phone and there you go, you've gotten past the gatekeeper. But often you might get back from the secretary, the gatekeeper, she'll say, excuse me, what's this in reference to? Well, what company are you calling from? What's your full name again? One of those questions. And no matter what the gatekeeper asks you, you're gonna give her a very short, one or two word answer and shut up again and create poisonous silence. What company from? Oh, XYZ, he hung all the company. What's in reference to? Well, it's personal business, not personal business. It's business related to him personally. The key here is poisonous silence, short answers, and you're calling from the perspective of the familiar. As soon as you are pegged out as being a salesperson, hi, John Smith, please, you're done. You'll never get past the gatekeeper. Now, just to be clear, what I'm talking about here is this high volume cold corn. We have a lot of leads to call. You make it a ton of calls, a hundred plus a day, and you're not spending a massive amount of time on any one particular lead because either your commission on the sale will not be high enough or you can have a lot more people to call. Now, if the situation where you actually had a limited amount of leads to call and each sale gave you a massive commission, well, that wouldn't be so quick to just slice in dice. In those cases, this slice in dice approach, honestly, it's incredibly effective and also it allows you to stay on a roll and they can call it, to call, to call. So please remember here, as we learn, sales is a numbers game and you've got to keep those numbers up. Now, one important point here with this strategy, when you're cold calling businesses, it is really hard to get through to the business owner, past the gatekeeper, unless you know that person's first name. So call them saying, hey, is the owner around? You can do that, but it's not nearly as effective as saying, hey, John, please, hey, can you see the John, please? Hey, is John there? You get a much better shot there because if you are a friend, you know the person's name. Now the mental philosophy here, when you don't know the name, hey, is the owner there, please? You want to have maybe a little bit more of an edge to your voice, like maybe you're a customer who's not quite disgruntled, but maybe they want to put a customer through. The point is you're not a salesperson. As soon as they think you're a salesperson, you're done. Now that being said, so you say, well, what if I don't have the name? Go buy some leads. You can get the names of most of the decision makers that you're calling. You just can. And if you can't, what you can also do, and this is better for high value business where each sale means a lot of money, you can actually use a two-step process where you actually spend the first call getting the name of the decision maker and then the second call calling back and trying to get through. That's for a very high target environment. In other words, each one's worth a lot of money. In addition to that, there's also the strategies of using the in-house switchboard to get to the decision maker. In other words, let's say you try to get to the decision maker and you just can't. You're cold calling. What you do is let's say your decision maker is a C-level person, hard to get through to. What you might do is call down two levels below to someone who always picks up their own phone. You pitch that person in a very broad way. They'll say, oh, well, you got to speak to the CEO about that. Oh, put your name again. You get that name. Now you call up the person's gatekeeper. You haven't gotten through yet. You say, hey, Jordan calling. John Smith from Over and Account told me to speak to Bill about the situation. He needs some help. I'm making up the words. But you see the point? You use an in-house referral and that's deadly effective. Now, conversely, you can also go the other way with this switchboard strategy, meaning let's say you're calling a middle tier executive. What you can do here is go above them to the office of the CEO. And while you might not get the CEO themselves on the phone, what you will always do is you'll get their gatekeeper. We're going to send you back down a level or two and now you actually have the authority of the CEO to go into this call with. That is powerful stuff. You see the one drawback of going the other way, meaning you go up to that, as you have authority when a low-level person refers to the CEO. Still better than nothing at all. However, just imagine this. You're trying to get to, let's say, a transportation manager at a logistics company. You call the boss, the very top guy. His gatekeeper gets on the phone. You tell her what you're looking for. She goes, oh, go see Jim Smith. He runs transportation. You say, thank you. You now pull back down. Say, hi, Jim. Jill Sanders over in the CEOs. I was told me to call you about, hello. You're getting through 100% of the time bottom line. Okay. Awesome. You sell face-to-face out in the field. Best place to start with a classic scene from a movie. Remember the movie Wall Street with Bud Fox is before the wolf of Wall Street, the real original Wall Street, right? Great scene. Bud is trying to get through to Gordon Gekko, the ultimate whale. He's calling every single day. Can't get through. He's trying everything. He's speaking to Natalie Gekko's gatekeeper. Befrenza is sweet, the Kajolza, she knows his name. He's trying everything. Can't do it. What does he do? He goes out on Gekko's birthday, gets a Cuban cigar box, right? They're illegal back then. They still are, actually. He goes to his office and just shows up. Boom. And he waits there. And he waits there. And finally, he meets Gekko. This is the kid. He calls me 59 days in a row. Wants to be a player. I ought to be a picture of you in the dictionary on a persistence kid. Yeah. Now, well, that's a movie. It's actually a very effective strategy, but you got to do it right. Now remember, when you show up, you got to use your body language principles at the highest level, big smile, eye contact, correct handshake. You're perceived that quarter of a second, but we're not talking about body language. We're talking about getting through gatekeepers. And the bottom line is, well, gatekeepers aren't royalty in those rare instances when the person is an utter whale and you really can get through to them and just make a fortune. Well, in that case, yeah, you want to do everything possible. So that strategy of getting to the gatekeeper face to face, giving the gift that she'll give to the boss, be some part too. Wow. That's a big one. But absent of that, and in the normal course of business, what you're really doing more than anything is barreling past the gatekeeper very often. Let me tell you what I mean. Now, this might strike you as shocking, but I'm a guy that made his living out in the field for many, many years before and after Wall Street. You know that when I was in the meat business, I was going to both homes and the businesses, warehouses, office buildings, strip malls. In a strip mall or office building, there'd be a secretary in front. You know what I would do? I'd walk in. I'd say, hi, how are you? And walk right past into the ocean. Grab your thing. Thank you. Say, hey, where's the CEO? Where's the manager? Right? And it's always back there. I'd walk right in. Hey, how you doing? Boom. Pitch the owner or the manager of the company, depending on what you want to speak to. Now, yeah, once in a while, they told me I had to leave, but far more often, they would respect it and actually get my pitch off. And I would get to people that no one else got through. Same thing goes for a warehouse situation. So I would actually enter through the trucking base. And people would hear the heart out they give you, and you'd walk in like you own the place. Now, obviously, you never want to do anything. That would embarrass your company. So be smart about this and always be ethical and elegant. But the point is, you're something wrong. Just walk against them. We're saying, hey, where's the CEO's office? I'm walking and boom. And you get right to them. Now, in homes, same thing goes sometimes, by the way, you go to a big home. Sometimes it's like the kid would answer the door. They say, oh, is mommy walking? Literally not too far. Now remember, OK, I was 22 years old and there's certain things about me. I'll be honest, right? I'm not a threatening looking guy. So people wouldn't call the police and flip out. So depending on how you look, if you're a big, giant, hoping person, you might not want to do that because the police take you away and times have changed a bit. But the point is, especially in businesses, is that once you get your foot in that actual door, just walk back there. For example, real estate agencies, you go to the front. There's 12 cards, right? All the brokers are independent. Grab all those cards, walk out, call a couple, get the speed. So if it still speaks to you or not, just walk right back in having names. Now you'll walk right in. So talk to all the brokers. Simple as that. Nothing stops you from going out there into the field and walking directly to the decision-maker. Now here's another great strategy. When you're out there in an area, and I always pick an area when I can this cold, right? I'll make my first pitch cold. Say I'm in a warehouse complex with lots of warehouses in a horseshoe and industrial book, right? My first pitch will be cold. But after that first pitch, I'll befriend that gatekeeper, the one in the front, and say, oh yeah, who else do you know in the area here? And they say, oh, I know Jimmy over here, Jim's from there. Next time I walk into the door, oh hey, Annabelle from over at XYZ company told me, and all of a sudden from a cold calling campaign, it becomes a referral campaign. So-and-so set me. And by doing that out in the field, what ends up happening is the people let you through because your referral. The gatekeeper is shh, full. So here's the deal. After all my years in selling, after spending 30 plus years teaching countless companies and countless salespeople how to get past gatekeepers when they're calling on the phone, the strategy that works better than anything else out there by a country mile is to use what I call ethical subterfuge to persuade the gatekeeper into thinking that you're either a personal friend of the decision maker or a current business associate. In a strip mall or office building, there'd be a secretary in front. You know what I would do? I'd walk in. I'd say, hi, how are you? And walk right past into the ocean. Grab me. I say, thank you. Now, obviously, you never want to do anything that would embarrass your company. So be smart about this and always be ethical and elegant. But the point is, you're something wrong. Just walk against them. We're saying, hey, where's the CEO's office? I'm walking in. Boom. And you get right to them. Now, in homes, same thing goes. Sometimes, by the way, you go to a big home. Sometimes it's like the kid would answer the door. They say, oh, is mommy? I walked in literally not too far. Now, remember, OK, I was 22 years old and there's certain things about me. I'll be honest, right? I'm not a threatening looking guy. So people wouldn't call the police and flip out. So depending on how you look, if you're a big, giant, hoping person, you might not want to do that because the police take you away and times have changed a bit. But the point is, especially in businesses, is that once you get your foot in that actual door, just walk back there. For example, real estate agencies, you go to the front. There's 12 cards, right? All the brokers are independent. Get all those cards, walk out, call a couple, get the speech. Sometimes they'll speak to you. If not, just walk right back in, having names. Now, you'll walk right in. So talk to all the brokers. Simple as that. Nothing stops you from going out there into the field and walking directly to the decision-maker. Now, here's another great strategy. When you're out there in an area, and I always pick an area when I can this cold, right? I'll make my first pitch cold. Say I'm in a warehouse complex with lots of warehouses in a horseshoe and industrial park, right? My first pitch will be cold. But after that first pitch, I'll befriend that gatekeeper, the one in the front, and say, oh, yeah, who else do you know in the area here? And they say, oh, I know Jimmy over here, Jim's from there. Next time I walk into the door, oh, hey, Annabelle from over at XYZ company told me, and all of a sudden from a cold calling campaign, it becomes a referral campaign. So-and-so set me. And by doing that out in the field, what ends up happening is the people let you through because you're a referral. The gatekeeper's full. So there's many different strategies for getting past gatekeepers. They differ in person and over the phone. Typically, it comes down to the familiar, I'm your friend, trying to get through another friend to stop that I'm a salesman trying to ram something down your throat, but also just be smart about it and never forget that all important overarching equation, which is the amount of time I spend trying to get through that gatekeeper. Could I just been done the phone more or walking in more doors? That's the question. How many people do you have on your list to call? How many people are there to walk in their door? If that number is vest and the amount of money you make in any particular sales, not life-changing, you've got to really question how much time you're going to spend trying to get past the gatekeeper versus slicing and dicing and bam, bam, bam, bam, bam, using the I'm the friend tonality. And I think you'll always went out 90% of the time doing that. Just remember, no matter what you hear out there, this information campaigns out there on the internet from supposed sales trainers, they say, oh, the gatekeeper should be treated like royalty. They are your best friends. Not a load of crap. It's just not true. Bottom line, these gatekeepers, nice people in personal life, I'm sure they have been placed there to thwart you. They're not your best friends. They're not your ally. They're not your confidant. They're there to stop you from getting through to their boss. Plain and simple. Now, that being said, does it mean you want to piss the gatekeeper off? No, of course not. I mean, you want to befriend them in the sense that, A, you're certainly not going to be mean to them and, B, by being pleasant and respectful, you can perhaps find out the best times to call, try to make an appointment. In other words, you can get the gatekeeper working for you and eventually, if you do this long enough, you might get them to actually put you through to the boss. But what you don't want to do here is you do not want to try to pitch the gatekeeper. You think that somehow you'll get them so hyped up, so certain that then they'll go run to their boss and you got to do this. Doesn't work that way. The best thing you could do is say to the gatekeeper, you know, I promise your boss can really use this. Please, if you all just take a second, what's the best time so you make it easy for them. But try to think you could pitch a gatekeeper that, my friend, is a dead end streak. Now one final point here before we go on to the next lesson here. It's been a long one, but there's a lot of info is that when you're in a two call system, meaning you send out leads, you get someone on the phone, you get past the gatekeeper, you got to the decision maker, you wrote it up as a lead. Now you try to call back. It's difficult sometimes. The way you could help get through that gatekeeper the second time is by using an ethical gift, meaning when you send out your information to that person, the lead, what we did back in the stratin days, we'd call someone up, get through with CEO. He'd say, sure, send me your information. But instead of just sending info, we would send him an actual book. Yay, thick. How to invest in common stocks. No one read it. But the point was that when it got there, dropped on their desk with weight, they saw this. And when you call back 10 days later, he said, oh, I'm the guy that sent you the book. And the gatekeeper was like, oh, hey, Mr. Smith, it's the guy that sent you that book. And I said, fine, put him through. Use reciprocity to get past the gatekeeper. I'll get to that in other parts of this training a couple of different times. We'll go through that. But that's all for now when it comes to getting past the gatekeeper. Obviously, there's a quiz here. There's a lot of info. I just kind of put it on strategy. You have to strategy because that's what it is. Just mix and match. Use what makes sense. Never do anything unethical. Just try it all. And believe me, if you do this, you will get through many, many gatekeepers that you have never gotten through before. See you in the next lesson. Bye.
